meta.description: Argument hypergraph for power-explained / The Common

depth_levels.0: Ontology — foundational claims about what exists and how
depth_levels.4: Case — specific historical instances and data

meta.note: depth nodes 0-1 are terrain (navigable but not destinations); entry nodes are audience on-ramps; edge_nodes and diagrams are hypergraph edges-as-nodes

nodes.label: Ontology
nodes.claim: The commons is the productive substrate of all value. Capture depends on what it cannot produce itself.

nodes.label: Philosophy & Theory
nodes.claim: Theoretical frameworks for understanding power, capture, and cooperation.

nodes.label: Structure
nodes.claim: Systemic and structural claims about how capture operates across domains.

nodes.label: Pattern
nodes.claim: Empirical patterns and mechanisms that recur across contexts.

nodes.label: Case & Data
nodes.claim: Specific historical instances, experiments, and data points.

nodes.label: For Urbanists
nodes.claim: The city is a commons — its value is collectively produced and privately captured.

nodes.label: For Libertarians
nodes.claim: The commons tradition predates the state — libertarians have been given the wrong history.

nodes.label: For Everyone
nodes.claim: Why everything costs so much and who benefits from that.

nodes.label: For Social Justice
nodes.claim: Racial capitalism, reproductive labor, and abolition are all enclosure problems.

nodes.label: For Tech Workers
nodes.claim: The internet was built on commons that are being enclosed. You are building the enclosure.

nodes.label: For Environmentalists
nodes.claim: Carbon markets are enclosures. The ecology is a commons. Indigenous governance is the prototype.

nodes.label: The Commons as Substrate
nodes.claim: Every capture mechanism depends on a productive substrate it cannot produce itself — the common.

nodes.label: Excess Beyond Capture
nodes.claim: The excess the common generates beyond what capture can contain is where alternatives live.

nodes.label: Enclosure Is Structural
nodes.claim: Enclosure is not a failure mode or aberration — it is the operating logic of capital.

nodes.label: Kropotkin: Mutual Aid
nodes.claim: Cooperation is as fundamental to evolution as competition — mutual aid is the baseline of human sociality, not the exception.

nodes.label: Gramsci: Hegemony
nodes.claim: Power persists not through force alone but through manufactured consent — hegemony makes the current order feel like common sense.

nodes.label: Ostrom: Governed Commons
nodes.claim: Commons do not inevitably collapse — they can be governed sustainably by communities with the right institutional rules.

nodes.label: Hardt & Negri: The Common
nodes.claim: Immaterial and affective labor produces a common wealth that capital appropriates but cannot generate — the multitude is the productive substrate.

nodes.label: Deleuze & Guattari: Capture
nodes.claim: The state apparatus captures flows — labor, land, code, affect — that were produced outside it. Capture is the primary operation of power.

nodes.label: Freire: Conscientization
nodes.claim: The oppressed internalize the logic of their oppression. Liberation requires naming the world — not receiving knowledge but producing it collectively.

nodes.label: Graeber: Care and Value
nodes.claim: The work that holds society together — care, maintenance, mutual aid — is systematically undervalued precisely because it cannot be enclosed.

nodes.label: Gödel: Every System Has an Outside
nodes.claim: Every formal system contains true claims it cannot prove from within. The outside is not a failure — it is constitutive.

nodes.label: Simondon: Technical Individuation
nodes.claim: Technology individuates — it carries its history and its associated milieu. Tools are not neutral; they encode the relations that produced them.

nodes.label: Hardin Was Wrong
nodes.claim: The tragedy of the commons describes enclosure, not commons — governed commons have survived for centuries. Hardin gave capitalism its alibi.

nodes.label: How Cities Get Captured
nodes.claim: Urban value is socially produced through collective life, infrastructure, and proximity — and systematically captured through land ownership and financialization.

nodes.label: Land Value Is a Commons Problem
nodes.claim: Rising land values reflect collective investment in neighborhoods — the gains flow to private owners, not the community that created them.

nodes.label: Racial Capitalism Is Enclosure
nodes.claim: Race was produced as a technology of enclosure — to sort who could be dispossessed, whose labor could be extracted without reciprocity.

nodes.label: Care Is the Economy
nodes.claim: Reproductive and care labor subsidizes the formal economy — its invisibility is not accidental, it is structurally necessary for profit to exist.

nodes.label: Carbon Markets Are Enclosures
nodes.claim: Carbon markets convert the atmospheric commons into a property regime — they don't solve the problem, they financialize it.

nodes.label: Money Must Be a Commons
nodes.claim: Money is a social technology — debt preceded markets, and the privatization of money creation is itself an enclosure.

nodes.label: Language Is a Commons
nodes.claim: Language is the original commons — collectively produced, impossible to enclose, the precondition of all other production.

nodes.label: The Internet Is Being Enclosed
nodes.claim: The internet was built on publicly funded research and open protocols — platform capitalism is the enclosure of that commons.

nodes.label: Hegemony Works Through Consent
nodes.claim: The current order persists because it has naturalized itself — making alternatives feel impossible is itself a form of power.

nodes.label: Underdevelopment Is a Verb
nodes.claim: Poor countries are not undeveloped — they were actively underdeveloped through colonial extraction of their commons.

nodes.label: The Party Ate the Revolution
nodes.claim: Vanguard parties that seize state power reproduce capture — the organizational form determines what the revolution can become.

nodes.label: The Reproductive Commons
nodes.claim: Women's bodies, reproductive labor, and care networks were the original enclosure target — primitive accumulation required their capture.

nodes.label: Abolition Is a Commons Project
nodes.claim: Abolition is not just dismantling prisons — it is building the commons institutions that make carceral capture unnecessary.

nodes.label: Indigenous Governance Is the Prototype
nodes.claim: Indigenous land governance systems are not primitive precursors — they are working models of commons stewardship that colonialism destroyed.

nodes.label: Open Data Isn't Enough
nodes.claim: Open data without governance is raw material for enclosure — transparency without power is a subsidy for those who can exploit it.

nodes.label: Mutual Aid Isn't Charity
nodes.claim: Mutual aid is horizontal reciprocity — it builds commons infrastructure. Charity is vertical and depoliticizing, it reproduces dependency.

nodes.label: Two Ways to Fix a Pothole
nodes.claim: Bureaucratic repair loops reset without compounding. Mutual aid repair compounds — knowledge, trust, and capacity accumulate across the network.

nodes.label: Why Your City Doesn't Work
nodes.claim: City dysfunction is not mismanagement — it is the predictable outcome of public investment being captured for private return.

nodes.label: Falling Through the Gap
nodes.claim: The gap between public service and private market is not a policy failure — it is a designed space where people in crisis are left.

nodes.label: How Linux Became the Internet
nodes.claim: Linux succeeded not despite being a commons but because of it — distributed contribution outcompeted proprietary development at scale.

nodes.label: Nobody Had To
nodes.claim: The most critical infrastructure of the internet was built by people who weren't paid to build it — voluntary commons production is real and massive.

nodes.label: Who Gets Paid
nodes.claim: Payment follows power, not contribution — the people whose work is most necessary are systematically the least compensated.

nodes.label: Who Gets to Die
nodes.claim: Risk distribution under capitalism is not neutral — exposure to death (environmental, medical, occupational) tracks the enclosure line.

nodes.label: Which Women
nodes.claim: Gender analysis without race and class reproduces the enclosure it claims to critique — liberation requires specifying whose commons is being built.

nodes.label: Enclosure and Extinction
nodes.claim: Biodiversity loss tracks enclosure — the conversion of ecological commons to private property destroys the governance relationships that sustained them.

nodes.label: Land Is the Foundation
nodes.claim: All other enclosures rest on land enclosure — access to physical space is the precondition of any commons.

nodes.label: Race and Enclosure
nodes.claim: Racial capitalism uses race to determine who can be dispossessed — it is not a variant of capitalism, it is capitalism's operating system.

nodes.label: Who Pays for the Next Shift
nodes.claim: Care work subsidizes every other form of labor — its invisibility is what makes the economy legible as profitable.

nodes.label: Skin in the Game
nodes.claim: The libertarian appeal to accountability through stake is correct — but stake in commons governance is what that actually looks like.

nodes.label: The Buried Libertarian Tradition
nodes.claim: There is a libertarian tradition of workers' self-governance — the CNT, mining cooperatives — that was buried when libertarianism was captured by capital.

nodes.label: The Tradition They Buried
nodes.claim: The commons tradition is not utopian — it is historical, continuous, and was actively suppressed because it worked.

nodes.label: The Commons Never Stopped
nodes.claim: Despite enclosure, commons production never stopped — it went underground, informal, digital, and mutual.

nodes.label: The Cooperative City
nodes.claim: Municipal cooperatives, community land trusts, and public banking are not radical experiments — they are working infrastructure in dozens of cities.

nodes.label: Rojava
nodes.claim: Rojava built a commons-based confederal system with women's liberation as constitutional structure — under active military siege.

nodes.label: The Zapatistas
nodes.claim: The Zapatistas have governed autonomous territories for thirty years using commons councils — without capturing the Mexican state.

nodes.label: Chile 2019
nodes.claim: The Chilean uprising won a constitutional referendum and lost the revolution — the institutional form absorbed the energy without transforming the structure.

nodes.label: Kerala
nodes.claim: Kerala achieved health and literacy outcomes comparable to wealthy nations through commons-based public investment — inside a capitalist state.

nodes.label: Occupy
nodes.claim: Occupy built commons infrastructure — kitchens, libraries, care — faster than the state could respond. It failed to persist, not to produce.

nodes.label: Törbel Alpine Pasture
nodes.claim: Törbel's alpine commons has been governed sustainably since 1483 — the rule: no more cattle than you can feed through winter.

nodes.label: Linux
nodes.claim: Linux runs most of the internet's infrastructure — built entirely through voluntary commons contribution, never enclosed.

nodes.label: Build the Commons
nodes.claim: The commons is not a relic — it is the horizon. The question is what to build, not whether to.

nodes.label: What We Do on Monday
nodes.claim: The transition requires concrete Monday-morning actions — institutional, relational, material — not just correct analysis.

nodes.label: Build It Now
nodes.claim: Don't wait for permission or the right conditions — build commons infrastructure in the present tense.

nodes.label: The Preparation Is Underway
nodes.claim: The commons is already being built — mutual aid networks, platform cooperatives, community land trusts — at a scale most people don't see.

nodes.label: The Commune Without the State
nodes.claim: Commune-scale governance is possible without state capture — the organizational form matters as much as the political program.

nodes.label: Commons Scenarios
nodes.claim: Three commons — Törbel pasture, Linux kernel, Valencia irrigation — showing how governed commons sustain themselves while enclosure extracts to collapse.

nodes.label: Two Ways to Fix a Pothole
nodes.claim: Bureaucratic repair loops reset without compounding. Mutual aid repair accumulates knowledge, trust, and pattern — the difference is visible in the graph.

nodes.label: Empirical refutation of enclosure alibi
nodes.claim: Ostrom's fieldwork directly falsifies Hardin's theoretical claim — the evidence is the argument.

nodes.label: Enclosure requires prior commons
nodes.claim: You cannot enclose what wasn't first a commons — enclosure is parasitic on collective production.

nodes.label: Capture operates through consent and flow
nodes.claim: Gramsci's hegemony and D&G's capture apparatus describe the same operation at different registers — ideological and material.

nodes.label: Race and gender as enclosure instruments
nodes.claim: Racial capitalism and reproductive enclosure are not parallel — they are co-constitutive mechanisms of the same primitive accumulation.

nodes.label: Urban value capture mechanism
nodes.claim: Land value is the primary mechanism by which urban commons are enclosed — proximity and collective investment become private wealth.

nodes.label: Platform enclosure of digital commons
nodes.claim: The same enclosure logic that fenced common land now fences common protocols — the move from open web to platform is enclosure.

nodes.label: Evolutionary basis of mutual aid
nodes.claim: Kropotkin's mutual aid thesis grounds the empirical pattern — cooperative behavior is not utopian, it is the default baseline.

nodes.label: Excess as the site of construction
nodes.claim: The commons the current order cannot fully capture is exactly where new institutions can be built — in the gap.

nodes.label: Commune form under pressure
nodes.claim: Rojava and the Zapatistas demonstrate that commune-scale governance is not just possible under favorable conditions — it is possible under siege.

nodes.label: Ecology requires commons governance
nodes.claim: Carbon markets fail where indigenous governance succeeds because ecology requires commons institutions, not property rights.

nodes.label: Care as the invisible commons substrate
nodes.claim: Graeber's value analysis and the care economy argument converge: the most necessary work is systematically invisibilized.

nodes.label: The outside is constitutive
nodes.claim: Gödel's incompleteness and the commons thesis share a structure: every system is sustained by what it cannot account for.

nodes.label: Myanmar: Full-Spectrum Refusal
nodes.claim: Myanmar's Civil Disobedience Movement built parallel commons institutions — healthcare, banking, governance — as an act of collective refusal of the coup state.

nodes.label: Shannon: Information as Commons
nodes.claim: Information wants to be transmitted without loss — Shannon's channel theory implies that enclosing information degrades the signal the whole system depends on.

nodes.label: The Bazaar Was Right, Politics Were Wrong
nodes.claim: Raymond's bazaar model correctly described open-source production but depoliticized it — the bazaar is a commons, not just a development methodology.

nodes.label: Turing and the Ungrateful State
nodes.claim: The universal machine was a commons gift — the state that built the computer on Turing's work then destroyed him. The pattern is the argument.

nodes.label: The Agent and the Commons
nodes.claim: AI agents are being built on commons knowledge but deployed as enclosure instruments — the agent is the new platform.

nodes.label: The Alibi Was a Mirror
nodes.claim: The justifications for enclosure — efficiency, stewardship, expertise — describe exactly what the commons does and what enclosure destroys.

nodes.label: The Human Was a Story
nodes.claim: The liberal individual — sovereign, rational, self-sufficient — is a fiction produced to justify enclosure. Humans are constitutively interdependent.

nodes.label: The Other and the Outside
nodes.claim: Capital requires an outside it can extract from — the Other is produced as the enclosable, the surplus population that subsidizes accumulation.

nodes.label: They Were Right About the Problem
nodes.claim: Libertarians, socialists, and anarchists all correctly diagnosed that something is being extracted — they disagree only on what and by whom.

nodes.label: What They Burned
nodes.claim: Enclosure required destroying the memory of commons — burning the records, enclosing the libraries, making the prior world unimaginable.

nodes.label: Your City Knows Less Than You Do
nodes.claim: Residents hold granular neighborhood knowledge that city systems cannot capture — the data gap is a governance gap.

nodes.label: Your City: A Power Analysis
nodes.claim: A practical framework for reading your own city's capture patterns — who owns the land, who controls the budget, where the commons is.
